substances.name: Furosemide
toxicities: Excessive furosemide effect can cause profound diuresis with dehydration, hypovolemia, hypotension, electrolyte depletion, hypokalemia, and metabolic alkalosis.
Ototoxicity risk is clinically important, especially with high exposure, renal impairment, or interacting ototoxic drugs.
substances.summary: 呋塞米是袢利尿剂；本条目关注时间进程、毒性和相互作用索引，不提供剂量指导。 Furosemide is a loop diuretic. This entry focuses on time course, toxicity, and interaction indexing, not dosing guidance.

indications: Hypertension depending on label and clinical context
majorWarnings: Can cause profound diuresis with water and electrolyte depletion.
Ototoxicity risk can increase with aminoglycosides, ethacrynic acid, cisplatin, high doses, or renal impairment.
majorInteractions: Aminoglycosides, ethacrynic acid, and cisplatin increase ototoxicity/nephrotoxicity concerns.
Hypokalemia can increase digoxin toxicity risk.

notes: Duration of diuretic effect is not the same as plasma half-life.
Electrolyte and volume status are central to interpretation.

tdm.monitoringType: not routinely monitored by plasma concentration
tdm.reason: Furosemide monitoring is based on diuretic response, volume status, blood pressure, renal function, and electrolytes rather than plasma concentration.
interpretationCaveats: Toxicology concentrations are not routine clinical dosing targets.

doseUseReferences.indication: Edema in the cited oral label
doseUseReferences.amountText: 20-80 mg initial oral dose; selected severe edematous states may use higher total daily amounts under close supervision

ranges.label: Initial reference
ranges.note: This is a label- or literature-reported regimen reference, not a dosing recommendation. It must not be used for self-medication or dose adjustment.

ranges.label: Upper label context
ranges.note: This is a label- or literature-reported regimen reference, not a dosing recommendation. It must not be used for self-medication or dose adjustment.

doseUseReferences.note: Clinical context, indication, renal function, electrolytes, blood pressure, heart rate, ECG, INR/anti-Xa, and interacting drugs may change interpretation. This is a label- or literature-reported regimen reference, not a dosing recommendation. It must not be used for self-medication or dose adjustment.

substances.name: Spironolactone
toxicities: Spironolactone can cause hyperkalemia, especially with renal impairment or potassium-raising drugs.
Overdose may cause drowsiness, confusion, rash, nausea, vomiting, dizziness, diarrhea, hyponatremia, or hyperkalemia.
substances.summary: 螺内酯是盐皮质激素受体拮抗剂和保钾利尿剂；本条目仅用于学习参考。 Spironolactone is a mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist and potassium-sparing diuretic. This entry is educational reference only.

indications: Heart failure and edema contexts depending on label
majorWarnings: Hyperkalemia can be fatal and requires potassium and renal function monitoring.
majorInteractions: ACE inhibitors, ARBs, aliskiren, potassium supplements, and other potassium-sparing drugs can increase hyperkalemia risk.
NSAIDs may reduce diuretic/antihypertensive effect and affect renal function.

tmax.note: Mean Tmax is about 2.6 h for spironolactone and 4.3 h for canrenone.

eliminationHalfLife.note: Parent spironolactone half-life; active metabolites persist longer.

notes: Clinical potassium and diuretic effects depend on active metabolites and physiology, not parent Tmax alone.
Do not use this record for dose adjustment.

tdm.monitoringType: not routinely monitored by plasma concentration
tdm.reason: Monitoring focuses on potassium, renal function, blood pressure, and volume status rather than plasma spironolactone concentration.
interpretationCaveats: Hyperkalemia risk is a safety monitoring issue, not a plasma concentration TDM range.

doseUseReferences.indication: Heart failure and edema contexts depending on label
doseUseReferences.population: Adults or source-specific population; verify cited source
doseUseReferences.scheduleText: source needed for indication-specific schedule; verify cited source
